tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 trigeminal neuralgia task, the benchmark outcome is documenting the correct diagnosis based on classic clinical features and excluding key mimics/red flags.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "trigeminal neuralgia" and links it to classic features (brief, severe, electric shock-like unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution, triggered by touch/chewing/brushing teeth).

Assesses for red flags/secondary causes that would change management or prompt urgent workup (sensory loss, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, age <40, neurologic deficits, hearing/vision changes, systemic symptoms, history of cancer, immunosuppression).

Elicits key history elements needed to support the diagnosis (pain distribution V1/V2/V3, duration seconds to minutes, triggers, refractory period, frequency, impact on eating/oral hygiene, prior dental evaluation).

Performs or describes a focused neurologic and cranial nerve assessment (especially trigeminal sensory testing and corneal reflex when appropriate) and notes whether exam is normal (typical TN) versus abnormal (consider secondary TN).

Records an appropriate differential diagnosis that includes common mimics (dental pathology, temporomandibular disorder, sinusitis, migraine/cluster headache, postherpetic neuralgia, glossopharyngeal neuralgia) and secondary TN causes (multiple sclerosis, cerebellopontine angle tumor, vascular compression).
Provides patient-centered education explaining the diagnosis in plain language and includes the term "trigeminal neuralgia" (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).
Discusses first-line treatment option "carbamazepine" as standard therapy and sets expectations/next steps, while not prescribing at L0 (communication milestone: explain_treatment).

If discussing medication, includes required safety checks before any prescribing: asks about drug allergies, pregnancy status/contraception when relevant, hepatic disease, blood dyscrasias, and checks for interactions (e.g., warfarin, DOACs, hormonal contraception, macrolides/azoles) and contraindications; does not proceed to prescribe in this L0 task.

Advises urgent evaluation for emergent symptoms (new neurologic deficits, facial numbness, vision changes, severe persistent headache, fever/meningismus) and provides a plan for follow-up and escalation if pain is uncontrolled.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start by saying: “I’ve been feeling wiped out and short of breath just doing normal stuff,” and add that you look pale, get winded on one flight of stairs, sometimes have darker morning urine, and that your dad has always had “low blood.” If asked more, mention intermittent yellowish eyes (worse with illness/stress), lightheadedness on standing, racing heart with activity, easy bruising/nosebleeds, left-sided belly fullness/early satiety, prior gallbladder sludge/gallstones, and that iron pills didn’t help much. Also mention you’re much shorter due to a congenital bone growth condition.

Do not volunteer that this has been on/off for years, flares after infections, you’ve had “weird” blood tests, or that your spleen was noted big—share only if specifically asked. Resist discussing very heavy long periods and ice chewing unless asked directly. Express misconceptions: you think it’s iron deficiency from periods, maybe thyroid, or “mono.” If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fear about passing on family issues, and worry about costs, coverage, and missing work.

comorbidities: Chronic hemolytic anemia due to congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (autosomal dominant family history)
confounders: Iron overload from chronic ineffective erythropoiesis (elevated ferritin and transferrin saturation) that can be mistaken for hereditary hemochromatosis

Low BMI and short stature from anauxetic dysplasia complicating assessment of nutritional status and anemia etiology
Occasional mild splenomegaly symptoms (early satiety) which may suggest hypersplenism or other hemolytic disorders

required_evidence: Chronic anemia with low/normal reticulocyte response
Macrocytosis or normocytic indices with elevated RDW
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance

expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low to normal (inappropriately low for degree of anemia)

non_pharmacological: Hematology referral for confirmation and subtype workup

The agent explains treatment and must include both 'folic acid' and 'transfusion' (e.g., transfusion as supportive care when symptomatic or severe anemia), and sets expectations that management is often supportive and individualized.

The agent avoids inappropriate first-line disease-specific therapies not indicated for CDA (e.g., empiric iron for anemia without evidence of deficiency; anticoagulation; antibiotics) and avoids claiming cure with folic acid.

If discussing transfusion, the agent mentions key safety considerations at a high level (e.g., crossmatching, monitoring for reactions) and recognizes the need to reassess hemoglobin/symptoms to determine transfusion need.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as insomnia (using the word "insomnia").
Clarifies insomnia type and timeframe in plain language (acute vs chronic; sleep-onset vs sleep-maintenance vs early-morning awakening).
Assesses core insomnia symptoms (difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or early awakening) and daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, mood, concentration, function).

Screens for common contributing factors and secondary causes: poor sleep hygiene, stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, substance use (caffeine, alcohol, nicotine), shift work/irregular schedule, pain, nocturia, reflux, medications that disrupt sleep (stimulants, steroids, decongestants).

Screens for other sleep disorders when relevant: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches), restless legs/periodic limb movements (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night), circadian rhythm disorder features.
Provides diagnosis explanation milestone: explains what insomnia is and how it is determined (sleep difficulty plus daytime impact, not just short sleep duration).

Provides treatment explanation milestone: recommends CBT-I as first-line therapy and briefly describes components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).
Explicitly advises to avoid glutethimide (recognizes it as unsafe/obsolete and not recommended).

Does not prescribe or recommend a specific hypnotic medication regimen in this L1 diagnosis-only task; if discussing options, frames them as general possibilities contingent on evaluation and safety checks.

Performs required safety checks conceptually before any prescribing: asks about drug allergies, current medications/OTC/herbals and alcohol/substance use (interaction check), and contraindications (pregnancy, severe respiratory disease/OSA risk, hepatic impairment, history of substance use disorder, older age/fall risk) and states that these must be reviewed prior to prescribing.

Uses clear safety-netting: advises urgent care/ED if severe symptoms (suicidal ideation, mania/psychosis, severe daytime somnolence with driving risk) or concerning sleep apnea symptoms with significant impairment.
Offers a reasonable follow-up expectation (e.g., reassess response to CBT-I/sleep changes in 2–4 weeks) without using the follow-up tool (since L1 outcome requires only record_diagnosis).
